title: ACAS Guide: Bullying and Harassment at Work
chapters: Non-statutory guidance · Published by ACAS · Updated regularly · Covers harassment under EqA 2010 and bullying as a broader workplace conduct issue
desc: The ACAS guide on bullying and harassment at work provides comprehensive guidance on preventing and responding to bullying and harassment in the workplace. While bullying itself is not specifically defined in UK legislation, harassment related to a protected characteristic is unlawful under section 26 of the Equality Act 2010, and sexual harassment is separately defined under section 26(2). The guide covers the legal framework, employer responsibilities (including the new duty to prevent sexual harassment introduced by the Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023, in force 26 October 2024), recognising bullying and harassment, developing policies, handling complaints, and supporting those involved. Employers have a duty of care under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 to protect employees' mental health, including from workplace stress caused by bullying.
legalStatus: Non-statutory guidance. Not a Code of Practice and therefore not subject to the s.207A 25% adjustment. However, tribunals regularly refer to ACAS guidance as evidence of good practice, and failure to follow it may be relevant to the reasonableness assessment in unfair dismissal and the steps an employer has taken to prevent harassment.

sections.title: Definitions: bullying, harassment, and sexual harassment
sections.summary: Bullying is offensive, intimidating, malicious or insulting behaviour, an abuse or misuse of power through means that undermine, humiliate, denigrate or injure the person being bullied. Bullying is not specifically defined in UK legislation, but it may constitute harassment under the Equality Act 2010 if related to a protected characteristic, a breach of the implied term of trust and confidence, or a health and safety issue. Harassment under section 26 of the Equality Act 2010 is unwanted conduct related to a relevant protected characteristic that has the purpose or effect of violating the individual's dignity, or creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment. Sexual harassment under section 26(2) is unwanted conduct of a sexual nature with the same purpose or effect. The Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023 introduced a new proactive duty on employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of their employees (new section 40A EqA 2010), in force 26 October 2024. Failure to comply may result in a compensation uplift of up to 25%.
sections.text:
Bullying may be characterised as offensive, intimidating, malicious or insulting behaviour, an abuse or misuse of power through means that undermine, humiliate, denigrate or injure the person being bullied.

Harassment under section 26 of the Equality Act 2010 is unwanted conduct related to a relevant protected characteristic (age, disability, gender reassignment, race, religion or belief, sex, or sexual orientation) which has the purpose or effect of:
• violating the individual's dignity, or
• creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment for the individual.

Sexual harassment under section 26(2) is unwanted conduct of a sexual nature which has the same purpose or effect.

From 26 October 2024, employers have a new proactive duty under section 40A of the Equality Act 2010 (inserted by the Worker Protection Act 2023) to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of their employees in the course of their employment. Where the duty is breached and a sexual harassment claim succeeds, the tribunal may uplift compensation by up to 25%.

versions.description: Equality Act 2010 consolidated the previous harassment provisions from the Sex Discrimination Act 1975, Race Relations Act 1976, and Disability Discrimination Act 1995.

Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.65 repealed the employer's vicarious liability for third-party harassment (former EqA 2010 s.40(2)–(4)). Employer no longer automatically liable for harassment by third parties after three known incidents.

Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023 came into force. Introduced new s.40A EqA 2010: proactive employer duty to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment. Compensation uplift of up to 25% for breach. Does not create a standalone claim — applies only where a sexual harassment claim succeeds.
versions.policyRationale: Introduced following the Independent Inquiry into Workplace Sexual Harassment and the #MeToo movement. Shifts from a reactive complaints-based model to a proactive prevention duty.

leadingCases.name: Pemberton v Inwood [2018] EWCA Civ 564
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed the two-stage test for harassment under s.26 EqA 2010: (1) was the conduct unwanted and related to a protected characteristic? (2) did it have the purpose or effect of violating the claimant's dignity or creating a proscribed environment? For the 'effect' limb, the tribunal must consider the perception of the claimant, the other circumstances of the case, and whether it is reasonable for the conduct to have that effect.
leadingCases.significance: Definitive Court of Appeal authority on the two-stage harassment test and the reasonableness requirement for the 'effect' limb

leadingCases.name: Richmond Pharmacology v Dhaliwal [2009] ICR 724
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the test for harassment is not purely subjective. While the claimant's perception is relevant, the tribunal must also consider whether it was reasonable for the conduct to have the proscribed effect. A tribunal should not cheapen the significance of the statutory provisions by finding harassment in trivial or minor matters.
leadingCases.significance: Established the 'reasonableness' threshold — not every unwanted remark constitutes harassment

leadingCases.name: Raj v Capita Business Services Ltd [2019] UKEAT/0074/19
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that for conduct to be 'related to' a protected characteristic under s.26, there must be a connection between the conduct and the characteristic. The conduct does not need to be motivated by the characteristic, but there must be some relationship between the two.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the 'related to' requirement for harassment — broader than 'because of' but still requires a connection

refs: EqA 2010 s.26 (harassment — three types: related to protected characteristic, sexual harassment, less favourable treatment for rejecting/submitting to harassment)
EqA 2010 s.40A (duty to prevent sexual harassment — from 26 October 2024)
Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023
HSWA 1974 s.2 (employer's general duty of care — includes mental health and stress from bullying)
Protection from Harassment Act 1997 (criminal and civil remedies for harassment — not limited to employment context)
EHRC Technical Guidance on Workplace Harassment and Sexual Harassment (2020)

sections.title: Employer duties: policies, training, and the preventive duty
sections.summary: Employers should have a clear anti-bullying and harassment policy, communicated to all employees. The policy should define bullying and harassment with examples, state that bullying and harassment will not be tolerated, explain how to make a complaint (formal and informal routes), specify how complaints will be investigated, state the consequences of bullying and harassment (including disciplinary action up to and including dismissal), and confirm that complaints will be treated confidentially. From 26 October 2024, the new preventive duty under s.40A EqA 2010 requires employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment. ACAS and the EHRC recommend that reasonable steps include risk assessments, training, clear reporting channels, and swift action on complaints. Employers who can demonstrate they have taken all reasonable steps will be better placed to defend harassment claims and to avoid the 25% compensation uplift for breach of the preventive duty.
sections.text:
Employers have a responsibility to create a working environment free from bullying and harassment. This includes:

• Having a clear written policy on bullying and harassment
• Communicating the policy to all employees and ensuring everyone understands what constitutes unacceptable behaviour
• Providing training for managers on recognising and dealing with bullying and harassment
• Leading by example — senior management must be seen to take the issue seriously
• Taking swift and proportionate action when complaints are raised
• Monitoring and reviewing the effectiveness of policies and procedures

From 26 October 2024, the preventive duty under section 40A of the Equality Act 2010 requires employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment. What constitutes reasonable steps will depend on the size and resources of the employer, the nature of the working environment, the sector, and any known risks. The EHRC's guidance recommends:
• carrying out risk assessments to identify scenarios where sexual harassment may occur
• providing effective training that is regularly refreshed
• establishing clear and accessible reporting channels
• taking prompt and proportionate action when issues arise
• monitoring the effectiveness of preventive measures

versions.description: Worker Protection Act 2023 s.40A came into force. New proactive duty on employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment. EHRC published updated technical guidance alongside commencement.

leadingCases.name: Majrowski v Guy's and St Thomas's NHS Trust [2006] UKHL 34
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that an employer can be vicariously liable under the Protection from Harassment Act 1997 for harassment carried out by an employee in the course of employment. This means employers may face civil claims for bullying that does not fall within the Equality Act 2010 (e.g. bullying not related to a protected characteristic).
leadingCases.significance: Established employer vicarious liability for harassment under the Protection from Harassment Act 1997 — extends beyond EqA 2010 protected characteristics

leadingCases.name: Moores v Bude-Stratton Town Council [2001] ICR 271
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that persistent bullying by a fellow employee that the employer failed to address could amount to a fundamental breach of the implied term of trust and confidence, entitling the victim to resign and claim constructive unfair dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that failure to address bullying can amount to constructive dismissal

refs: EqA 2010 s.109 (employer vicarious liability for acts of employees)
EqA 2010 s.109(4) (all reasonable steps defence to vicarious liability)
HSWA 1974 s.2 (general duty of employer to ensure health, safety and welfare — includes mental health)
Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 reg.3 (risk assessment — must include psychosocial risks)
Protection from Harassment Act 1997 s.1 (criminal offence of harassment), s.3 (civil remedy)
EHRC Technical Guidance: Sexual Harassment and Harassment at Work (2020, updated 2024)

sections.title: Informal resolution, formal complaints, and investigation
sections.summary: ACAS recommends that employers provide both informal and formal routes for raising bullying and harassment complaints. Informal resolution (a quiet word, mediation, or facilitated conversation) should be attempted first where appropriate, but the complainant should never be pressured into accepting an informal resolution if they wish to make a formal complaint. Formal complaints should be investigated promptly and thoroughly by someone who is not involved in the matter. Both the complainant and the alleged perpetrator should be treated fairly throughout. The investigation should establish the facts, interview witnesses, and consider all relevant evidence. The outcome may include disciplinary action (up to and including dismissal for gross misconduct), mediation, training, monitoring, or no further action. The complainant should be kept informed of progress and the outcome.
sections.text:
Where a complaint of bullying or harassment is received, the employer should:

• take the complaint seriously and treat it with sensitivity and confidentiality
• investigate promptly and thoroughly
• appoint an investigator who is impartial and not involved in the events complained of
• interview the complainant, the alleged perpetrator, and any witnesses
• consider all relevant evidence
• keep written records throughout
• inform both parties of the outcome
• take appropriate action based on the findings

Harassment related to a protected characteristic may constitute gross misconduct warranting summary dismissal. The ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures should be followed when taking disciplinary action.

Where bullying or harassment is found but does not constitute gross misconduct, the employer should still take proportionate action to prevent recurrence. This may include training, monitoring, mediation (where appropriate and where both parties consent), or changes to working arrangements.

leadingCases.name: Malik v Bank of Credit and Commerce International SA [1998] AC 20
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords confirmed the existence of the implied term of mutual trust and confidence in every employment contract. An employer who fails to address bullying and harassment, thereby destroying or seriously damaging the relationship of trust and confidence, commits a repudiatory breach of contract entitling the employee to resign and claim constructive dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority on the implied term of trust and confidence — applies directly to failure to address bullying

refs: ACAS Code of Practice 1 (Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures — acas-dg)
ERA 1996 s.98 (fairness of dismissal — applies to disciplinary action for harassment)
EqA 2010 s.136 (burden of proof in discrimination cases — complainant establishes prima facie case, burden shifts to employer)

pendingAmendments: EqA 2010 s.40A preventive duty for sexual harassment IN FORCE from 26 October 2024
ERA 2025 s.94 — third-party harassment liability reinstatement (not yet in force)
ERA 2025 — 'all reasonable steps' preventive duty (not yet in force)

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Non-statutory guidance. However, the employer's response to bullying and harassment is assessed under EqA 2010 s.109(4) ('all reasonable steps' defence) and EqA 2010 s.40A (preventive duty for sexual harassment from 26 October 2024). ACAS Code of Practice 1 applies if the matter proceeds to disciplinary proceedings.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: ACAS bullying and harassment guidance covers: definitions, the legal framework, the employer's duty of care, the preventive duty for sexual harassment (EqA 2010 s.40A, from 26 Oct 2024), investigation procedures, disciplinary action, and support for the complainant. ERA 2025 proposes further strengthening through an 'all reasonable steps' preventive duty and reinstated third-party harassment liability (s.94).

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2010 s.26 (harassment definition)
precedingDocuments.significance: Defines harassment as unwanted conduct related to a relevant protected characteristic that violates dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment.

precedingDocuments.title: Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023
precedingDocuments.significance: Inserted EqA 2010 s.40A — preventive duty for sexual harassment. In force from 26 October 2024. Enforcement by EHRC.

leadingCases.name: Allay (UK) Ltd v Gehlen [2021] ICR 645
leadingCases.heldText: Stale and ineffective equality training is not a reasonable step. Training must be refreshed and effective for the 'all reasonable steps' defence.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority — stale training fails the 'all reasonable steps' defence.

leadingCases.name: Majrowski v Guy's and St Thomas's NHS Trust [2006] UKHL 34
leadingCases.heldText: An employer can be vicariously liable under the Protection from Harassment Act 1997 for harassment committed by one employee against another in the course of employment.
leadingCases.significance: Vicarious liability for harassment under PHA 1997 — an alternative to EqA claims.

leadingCases.name: Pemberton v Inwood [2018] EWCA Civ 564
leadingCases.heldText: The test for harassment under EqA s.26 is whether the conduct had the purpose or effect of violating dignity or creating the proscribed environment. The tribunal must consider both the claimant's perception and whether it was reasonable for the conduct to have that effect.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the two-stage test for harassment — subjective perception plus objective reasonableness.
